questions.question: A team wants to improve a foundation model's answers for their use case and is weighing different customization approaches by cost. Ordering from least to most expensive, which sequence is correct?

choices.text: Prompt engineering, RAG, instruction fine-tuning, domain adaptation.
choices.explanation: Generally the cheapest improvement is prompt engineering, followed by RAG, then instruction-based fine-tuning, and finally domain adaptation fine-tuning, which is the most resource intensive. This ordering helps teams try lower-cost options first.

choices.text: Instruction-based fine-tuning, then domain adaptation fine-tuning, then prompt engineering, then RAG.
choices.explanation: This places the most expensive fine-tuning approaches first and the cheapest approaches last, which is the opposite of the recommended cost ordering.

choices.text: RAG, then prompt engineering, then domain adaptation fine-tuning, then instruction-based fine-tuning.
choices.explanation: Prompt engineering is typically cheaper than RAG, and instruction-based fine-tuning is usually less costly than domain adaptation fine-tuning, so this sequence is incorrect.

choices.text: Domain adaptation fine-tuning, then instruction-based fine-tuning, then RAG, then prompt engineering.
choices.explanation: This reverses the correct order. Fine-tuning approaches are more expensive than RAG and prompt engineering, so starting with domain adaptation fine-tuning is the costliest, not the cheapest, path.

questions.question: A financial services company needs to extract printed text, handwriting, form fields, and tables from scanned loan application PDFs. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

choices.text: Amazon Lex, which builds conversational chatbots.
choices.explanation: Amazon Lex creates chatbots with intents and slots. It has no document extraction capability.

choices.text: Amazon Textract, which extracts text, forms, and tables from documents.
choices.explanation: Amazon Textract is a document AI service that extracts printed text, handwriting, form key-value pairs, and tables from scanned PDFs and images. It is the right choice for processing loan application documents.

choices.text: Amazon Translate, which translates text between languages.
choices.explanation: Amazon Translate converts text from one language to another. It does not extract text or tables from scanned documents.

choices.text: Amazon Comprehend, which analyzes the meaning of text such as sentiment and entities.
choices.explanation: Amazon Comprehend interprets text that already exists in machine-readable form. It does not extract text and structure from scanned images; that is Textract's job.

questions.question: An artist wants to generate original images from text descriptions. Which type of model is most commonly used for high-quality text-to-image generation?

choices.text: K-means clustering, which groups unlabeled data into clusters.
choices.explanation: K-means is an unsupervised clustering algorithm. It organizes data into groups and does not create new image content.

choices.text: Linear regression models, which fit a straight-line relationship between variables.
choices.explanation: Linear regression predicts a continuous numeric value from inputs. It cannot generate images.

choices.text: Decision trees, which split data on feature thresholds to make predictions.
choices.explanation: Decision trees are interpretable models for classification and regression. They are not used to generate novel images from text.

choices.text: Diffusion models, which generate images by reversing a noise process.
choices.explanation: Diffusion models are widely used for image generation. During training noise is added to images, and during generation the model reverses that noise into an image guided by a text prompt.

questions.question: A team wants to detect bias in their training data and trained model, and also produce explainability reports showing how features influence predictions. Which Amazon SageMaker capability supports this?

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Model Registry, which versions and approves model artifacts.
choices.explanation: Model Registry tracks model versions, metadata, and approval status. It is a governance and lifecycle tool, not a bias or explainability analysis tool.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Pipelines, which orchestrates CI/CD workflows for ML.
choices.explanation: Pipelines automate ML workflow steps such as data prep, training, and deployment. They do not perform bias detection or explainability analysis themselves.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store, which stores and reuses ML features.
choices.explanation: Feature Store centralizes and reuses features across models. It does not analyze bias or generate explainability reports.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Clarify, for bias detection and explainability.
choices.explanation: SageMaker Clarify detects bias in datasets and models, supports foundation model evaluation, and provides explainability such as feature importance. It is the dedicated capability for fairness and explainability analysis.

questions.question: A user submits input designed to override an application's system instructions and make a foundation model ignore its safety rules. Which of the following are effective mitigations against prompt injection? (Select all that apply.)

choices.text: Use guardrails and content filters to detect and block malicious or policy-violating input and output.
choices.explanation: Guardrails and content filters add a defensive layer that can catch harmful or manipulative content before it reaches the model or before responses are returned, helping mitigate injection attempts.

choices.text: Allow the model to call approved tools without validating each requested action once the user is authenticated.
choices.explanation: Authentication alone does not make every model-requested action safe. Prompt injection can still manipulate tool use, so sensitive actions should be validated against policy and user intent before they execute.

choices.text: Give the model broad read access to internal systems so it has enough context to answer most requests.
choices.explanation: Broad access may improve coverage, but it increases the impact of a successful prompt injection. The safer pattern is least privilege: provide only the tools and data needed for the task and validate their use.

choices.text: Keep system instructions separate from user input and add explicit instructions to ignore unrelated or malicious content.
choices.explanation: Separating trusted system instructions from untrusted user input, and explicitly instructing the model to disregard attempts to override its rules, reduces the effectiveness of prompt injection attacks.

choices.text: Limit the model's access to sensitive tools and data, and validate tool actions before they execute.
choices.explanation: Applying least privilege to the tools and data a model can reach, and validating actions before executing them, limits the damage an injection attempt can cause even if some malicious input gets through.

questions.question: A compliance team wants to automatically discover and report on sensitive data such as personally identifiable information (PII) stored in Amazon S3 buckets. Which AWS service is designed for this?

choices.text: Amazon Inspector, which scans for software vulnerabilities in EC2, ECR images, and Lambda.
choices.explanation: Amazon Inspector finds software and network vulnerabilities in workloads. It does not discover or classify sensitive data in S3 buckets.

choices.text: AWS Trusted Advisor, which gives account-level best-practice recommendations.
choices.explanation: Trusted Advisor offers recommendations across cost, security, performance, and more, but it does not specifically discover and classify PII inside S3 objects.

choices.text: AWS Artifact, which provides downloadable compliance reports and agreements.
choices.explanation: AWS Artifact is a portal for compliance documents such as SOC and ISO reports. It does not scan data stores for sensitive content.

choices.text: Amazon Macie, which discovers sensitive data such as PII in S3.
choices.explanation: Amazon Macie uses machine learning to discover, classify, and report on sensitive data such as PII stored in S3, supporting data privacy and governance requirements.

questions.question: A team must predict the exact future temperature in degrees for each day next week using historical weather data. Is this a classification or a regression problem, and why?

choices.text: Reinforcement learning, because an agent earns rewards for accurate forecasts.
choices.explanation: Reinforcement learning trains an agent through actions and rewards in an environment. Forecasting a numeric value from historical labeled data is a supervised regression problem.

choices.text: Classification, because the model assigns each day to a discrete category.
choices.explanation: Classification predicts categories such as 'hot' or 'cold,' not exact numeric values. Since the target is a precise temperature, this is regression, not classification.

choices.text: Regression, because the target is a continuous numeric value.
choices.explanation: Predicting a continuous numeric quantity such as temperature in degrees is a regression task. Classification predicts discrete categories, which does not match a continuous output.

choices.text: Clustering, because the data is unlabeled and grouped automatically.
choices.explanation: Clustering groups unlabeled data by similarity. The task uses labeled historical values to predict a number, so it is supervised regression, not clustering.

questions.question: A company wants to adapt a copy of a foundation model using their own labeled prompt-and-completion examples so the model better matches their tone and domain. The base model's weights should be updated in this adapted copy. Which approach are they using?

choices.text: Prompt engineering, which changes the wording of prompts without altering model weights.
choices.explanation: Prompt engineering improves results by crafting better prompts, but it does not change the model's weights. The scenario explicitly updates weights, which is fine-tuning.

choices.text: Fine-tuning, which adapts a model copy and changes its weights.
choices.explanation: Fine-tuning trains a copy of a foundation model on your own data, updating its weights so it better fits your domain and style. Supervised fine-tuning specifically uses labeled prompt/completion examples.

choices.text: Retrieval Augmented Generation, which adds external context at query time without changing weights.
choices.explanation: RAG supplies retrieved context to the model at inference time and leaves the model's weights unchanged. The scenario describes updating weights, so this is fine-tuning.

choices.text: Tokenization, which converts text into tokens for processing.
choices.explanation: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into tokens. It is a preprocessing step, not a method for adapting a model to a domain.

questions.question: A contact center wants to transcribe recorded customer calls into text, automatically redact PII from the transcripts, and handle domain-specific terms. Which AWS service should they use?

choices.text: Amazon Rekognition, which analyzes images and video.
choices.explanation: Amazon Rekognition is a computer vision service for images and video. It does not transcribe audio recordings into text.

choices.text: Amazon Polly, which converts text into lifelike speech.
choices.explanation: Amazon Polly performs text-to-speech, the opposite of what is needed. The task is to convert recorded speech into text.

choices.text: Amazon Comprehend, which analyzes the meaning of existing text.
choices.explanation: Amazon Comprehend analyzes text once it exists, but it does not transcribe audio. Transcribe is required to turn the call recordings into text first.

choices.text: Amazon Transcribe, a speech-to-text service with PII redaction.
choices.explanation: Amazon Transcribe converts speech to text and offers features such as PII redaction, custom vocabularies for domain-specific words, and language identification, matching all the contact center's needs.

questions.question: Under the AWS shared responsibility model, which of the following is the customer's responsibility when using managed AI services such as Amazon Bedrock?

choices.text: Managing data, access permissions, and application configuration.
choices.explanation: In the shared responsibility model, customers are responsible for security in the cloud: their data, IAM permissions, network and application configuration, and encryption choices. AWS secures the underlying infrastructure.

choices.text: Operating the global network backbone that connects AWS Regions.
choices.explanation: The global network infrastructure connecting AWS Regions is owned and operated by AWS, not the customer.

choices.text: Maintaining the physical security of AWS data centers and hardware.
choices.explanation: Physical security of data centers and hardware is AWS's responsibility (security of the cloud), not the customer's.

choices.text: Patching the underlying hypervisors and managed service infrastructure.
choices.explanation: Patching and maintaining the underlying managed infrastructure and hypervisors is handled by AWS as part of security of the cloud.
